problem_statement: Agentic AI systems generate actions dynamically from model inference rather than fixed code paths, creating an unbounded action surface that traditional access control (RBAC, allow-lists) and loosely-coupled LLM guardrails cannot govern; the paper addresses how to enforce organizational and regulatory policy over these model-driven actions at runtime.
magnitude_evidence: Baseline system without the policy engine had only 47% of outcomes with complete lineage and 12% initial false positives; policy engine reduced unsafe or non-compliant actions by approximately 68-73% (avg 71%), as judged by human reviewers
insufficiency_evidence: Existing guardrails 'rarely encode organizational policy, regulatory thresholds, or cross-action invariants, and they lack a unifying runtime semantics'; single-signal safety systems fail (e.g., prompt-injection detectors 'underperform on indirect (second-hop) attacks'; PII classifiers 'may under-detect derived or inferential privacy leaks'); individual classifiers 'exhibit correlated error patterns'
solution_mechanisms: runtime policy engine on control path of every governed action
